1) What's there to explain? The only people that drink this is children, pregnant women, and recovering alcoholics. All people that would be drinking regular beer if they could. 

2) One glass of wine is definitely enough to make most people tipsy, and is probably enough to get my mom drunk. These people you're probably talking about are parents, who want to be tipsy enough that they aren't sober but not drunk enough that they can't complete their familial obligations. 

3) Using alcohol for cooking has a completely different purpose, which is to enhance other flavors. We add acids to rich dishes to balance the flavors, but no one is drinking balsamic vinegar by the glass, are they?
http://www.seriouseats.com/2013/03/ask-the-food-lab-does-vodka-sauce-really-need-vodka.html
tl;dr the vodka is used to enhance other flavors not to provide a flavor of it's own.

1) Then please explain non-alcoholic beer. ETA: yeah, not a lot of people drink it, but some do. 

2) People frequently have, for example, one glass of wine with dinner- which for most people is not enough alcohol in a short enough period of time to experience any effects. Why, if everyone is just drinking for the effects?

3) Frequently, wine, beer, and liquor are used in cooking. It is often in such a way that either most of the alcohol is burned off or evaporated, or in such small quantities that no one eating the food would experience the effects. Again, if alcohol all tastes gross and everyone is using it for its effects, why would it be used in that way?

ETA: People eat lots of things that provide no nutrition. For example diet sodas. Some people like the taste of some sodas and hate others. But that can change over time. Nutrition or lack thereof has exactly 0 impact on what tastes people enjoy. 


Your whole view is based on the idea that you know what people feel/like better than they do themselves. The best counter to the idea that no one likes the taste of any alcohol ever is someone saying "Actually, I do." Except your response is- you're lying. I'm not really sure how you can think you know better than everyone ever what tastes they enjoy.

I agree with your last paragraph. Studying or pointing out biological differences between groups of people does not constitute racism. Identifying difference is a matter of finding the truth, dealing with truth is a topic of ethics. Racism is a problem of the latter area. I would like to shift your view in two aspects.

#The significance of the genetic component

It is important to note the difference between a trait being inheritable and the a trait being "genetic". When looking at the studies of complex human traits, you are unlikely to find an isolated genetic component, but rather [the trait's heritability is studied instead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heritability_of_IQ). Heritability of IQ has the "overwhelming evidence", which you are talking of.

First caveat with heritability is that laypeople don't really know what it means. Quote from Robert Sapolsky's *Behave*:

> What's a heritability score? "What does a gene do?" is at least two questions. How does a gene influence average levels of a trait? How does a gene influence variation among people in levels of that trait?

> These are crucially different. For example, how much do genes have to do with people's scores averaging 100 on this thing called an IQ test? Then how much do genes have to do with one person scoring higher than another?

> Or how much do genes help in explaining why humans usually enjoy ice cream? How much in explaining why people like different flavors?

> These issues utilize two terms with similar sounds but different meanings. If genes strongly influence average levels of a trait, that trait is a strongly inherited. If genes strongly influence the extent of variability around that average level, that trait has a high heritability. **It is a population measure, where a heritability score indicates the percentage of total variation attributable to genetics.**

> The difference between an inherited trait and heritability generates at least two problems that inflate the putative influence of genes. First, people confuse the two terms (things would be easier if heritability were called something like "gene tendency"), and in a consistent direction. People often mistakenly believe that if a trait is strongly inherited, it's thus highly inheritable. And it's particularly bad that confusion is typically in that direction, because people are usually more interested in variability of traits among humans than in average levels of traits. For example it's more interesting to consider why some people are smarter than others than why humans are smarter than turnips.

Second caveat is that there are forms of inheriting a trait, that don't have much to do with genetics, but rather fall into the field of [epigenetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics). For example it is possible to inherit traits from your mother due to the hormonal influence in the womb - your mother being stressed during pregnancy exposes you to stress hormones, which make you more likely to be sensitive to stress as an adult, which makes a woman more likely to be stressed during pregnancy etc. There are a lot more caveats, but let's leave the formal critique at that.

More important than dissecting the heritability part is to look at the significance of it as a whole, because as much influence as genes may have, for some parts of IQ it is dwarfed by other factors. Let's look at the [Flynn Effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flynn_effect):

> The Flynn effect is the substantial and long-sustained increase in both fluid and crystallized intelligence test scores measured in many parts of the world over the 20th century. When intelligence quotient (IQ) tests are initially standardized using a sample of test-takers, by convention the average of the test results is set to 100 and their standard deviation is set to 15 or 16 IQ points. When IQ tests are revised, they are again standardized using a new sample of test-takers, usually born more recently than the first. Again, the average result is set to 100. However, when the new test subjects take the older tests, in almost every case their average scores are significantly above 100.

Genes don't shift rapidly enough to explain that phenomenon. More importantly genes never work in isolation. Consider that the generational upwards trend is higher than the variability between groups inside the same generation. Read another way: You should worry about not having people's water sources being poisoned with lead before ever worrying about race when talking about IQ.

#The nature of the "racist" counter argument

The "Left" has a long tradition of being sceptical of biology to the point of denying science _when they are opposed to perceived implications_.[1]

That is why some people on the Left still defend the blank slate theory and discard every fact pointing to human nature not being completely malleable. The perceived implication is that this would somehow make things like racism, sexism, or classism ok.

Instead of focusing on the separation of science and ethics (i.e. attacking the perceived implication via moral argument), an intolerable implication leads to the denial of science.

The "racist" counter argument is not meant to be a counter argument to the content, but to the perceived implication.


[1] Peter Singer in _A Darwinian Left_ elaborates on the Left's opposition to the theory of evolution. Steven Pinker in _The Blank Slate_ discusses the adherence to theories like the blank slate or the noble savage despite opposing evidence. The "Right" shows the same problem, but for different perceived implications.

Voter ID laws are a Republican voter suppression effort. They solve no problem and are not necessary for anything. They also have nothing to do with voting machines.

[Republican politicians admit voter ID laws are a suppression effort.](https://www.thedailybeast.com/north-carolina-gop-brags-racist-voter-suppression-is-workingand-theyre-right)

I will tell you that on one part, I completely agree with you. There are digital voting systems out there without checks and balances to ensure the votes are recorded accurately. I am dubious of 'trusting' digital machines without audit trails. It was reported after the last 2016 election recounts were not possible in some places because the machines did not have backup counts or other means to save votes.

BUT, I can also tell you that this is not a 'Republican' conspiracy. If anything, a stronger argument could be made that it is a DNC conspiracy based on the staunch opposition to voter ID laws. message:
Something no one here has touched on but should really be examined.  The cost of the system you are proposing.

Assuming that we could slim down our healthcare offerings to match what Canada does and we could have the same number of physicians while lowering their salaries to be similar to Canada, we would have to massively increase our healthcare system.  While we have a little more landmass than Canada, we have a far larger population, of which it is scattered across the nation.  In Canada, because of the climate, you often don't find people living in about a third of their total land mass.

From a cost perspective, we simply have too much land, spread among too many people to make a single payer option like that affordable.  We already spend more than Canada for Medicare (which reimburses hospitals and doctors below the cost of treatment for many procedures) to cover less people.  Can you imagine the cost of expanding that to everyone?  But let's assume that we don't expand medical coverage out.  Currently, about 60 million people (roughly 18% of the US population).  We spend roughly 700 billion a year.  Now assuming that we can insure the rest of the US, without any increase in usage or cost, that means we would end up spending 3.5 trillion a year (about 200 billion more than we spend right now total in both public and private options).  This doesn't include the administration costs, the government workers, legislation and compliance....Just the payouts from the system.  Now think about how many more people would consume more when there is no cost to them.  No deductibles, no major costs out of pocket.  We'd see an increase.  Let's assume it's a modest 10%.  That means we'd be spending a half a trillion dollars more per year than we spend as a society right now.  And that's assuming a 10% increase in usage.  Most estimates place it around 40-50%.  

Can you imagine how crippling to an economy taxing 5 trillion dollars would be?  That's a little more than 2 trillion more than the total taxes collected today.  Even if we cut every single thing that wasn't healthcare payouts, we wouldn't be able to pay for it.

But let's not stop there.  Remember how the doctors get paid?  It's usually not enough.  So we would have to increase reimbursements for a lot of conditions.  Meaning there are cost increases there.  Plus cost increases for placing more hospitals in places where we didn't have them before as private facilities close or are not in serviceable areas.

People see universal healthcare and think it is great.  But it only works when you have populations in concentrated areas, and small populations.  When you start expanding it out to large land masses and large populations, it falls apart very quickly.

We are not biologically herbivores. We cannot get all of our nutritional needs from plant sources that grow in one area together. You either have to take supplements for iron, calcium, and B12 as well as several other vitamines and complete proteins. 

It is a fluke of modernity that it is possible to import various plant stuffs from all over the world, and make supplements to give you what you would normally get from meat but it is not at all natural. 

We are meant to eat meat (though less than we typically do) in addition to plants. We are omnivores and you can tell this by the types of bacteria in our guts and the type of digestive tracts we have (we do not have large fermentation chamber(s) for digesting plant material, nor the bacteria to get the vitamins we need from them).

It's not for no reason, it's a way to get delicious and healthy food for not too much. My dad would bring home a few months worth of steak and sausages for the cost of a car trip and a few bullets. 


Frankly it boils down to how much value you give an animal life. I like animals, and I don't want to cause entirely unnecessary suffering. But at the same time I can't see any ethical reason to not eat them, it's entirely natural, though as higher thinking animals ourselves we can do the kindness of giving them a pleasant life and a quick sudden death, which is frankly a lot more than they could usually expect in the wild.

>	You can say the exact same thing about murder, yet I think most people would agree that murder is pretty easy to define, certainly for common parlance.

No, murder and racism are two wildly different concepts.

First of all there is no academic debate on what murder is yet there’s a robust one on the definition of “race” the history of the term and concept and what race actually entails.

Is Islam a race? Is being bigoted towards Islamic people racism? Is “black” a race? Is “white?” In the original post OP describes being racist to white people is racism, but how is “white” a race? **what** is a race?

Race was first used to denote speakers of a certain language, then national affiliations. By the 17th century it was used to describe phenotypical traits. Modern scholars consider race a social construct, that while partially based on biological traits is not an inherent physical or biological quality.

Thats not even an in-depth discussion. how on earth is that similar in any way to murder which is as cut and dry as it gets.

The comparison makes no sense.

The dictionary says race is

“a group of persons related by common descent or heredity.”

You don’t need to drown yourself in nuance to see that this misses the mark by quite a bit. It’s also worth noting that definitions change and evolve all the time.

Dictionary says racism is

“a belief or doctrine that inherent differences among the various human racial groups determine cultural or individual achievement, usually involving the idea that one's own race is superior and has the right to dominate others or that a particular racial group is inferior to the others.”

“Beliefs and doctrines” are sets of ideas. So by it’s very nature you can’t effectively define a doctrine with one sentence. You can use the dictionary definition to describe Christianity or Liberalism but to use the strict definitions of the words in the absence of nuance would be silly.

I could go on abkht this but my point is that you shouldn’t misuse a dictionary definition for such a complicated concept as racism.

>	Whether you like it or not, the vast majority of people believe that institutional racism and racism are different things, with the latter thing being something that individuals can do on their own.
So that for example a Zimbabwean white can think that "all these n*ggers are fucking worthless" (including Obama etc) while fleeing the state run murderers in a country where he has zero institutional power, after having seen his wife raped and murdered.
Is he racist? Of course he is. Is he wielding institutional power to keep black people down? No, literally the opposite is happening.
Elevating this to "but white people have more

See this is where we disagree. And where many do.

First off there are many types of racism. One being implicit bias. Which is heavily socially constructed. These affect everyone of a specific racial group for historical and social reasons. By this person perpetuating racist views about a group that fuel negative perceptions and implicit bias he is actually contributing to a larger and more nefarious system of racism that affects black people.

So yes he is wielding institutional power even if it’s not as obvious as literal institutional racism like laws against property ownership for one race. 

>	Elevating this to "but white people have more institutional power globally, hence he can't be racist" strikes the vast majority of people as completely inane

Fast majorly of people? You like to throw that around a lot. If your argument is strong it should stand on it’s own without these kinds of anecdotes. And if it is critical to your argument provide a source otherwise why add it? I don’t make a habit of accepting broad statements like that as anything but an attempt to disguise a view as more relevant than it actually is.

Also this statement is a bit confusing to read. I don’t really get what you’re trying to say or who is making those claims.

>	one of the easiest ways for Trump to gain votes. If he has any sense, the last 6 months before the presidential election will rotate around this topic (and whether people who break the law are criminals) and we'll have to tolerate another 4 years of him because democrats just can't stop being absolutely insane about these two topics.

I think democrats and liberals need to work on their messaging so as not to be so hostile to white people. Not as a whole but for some. The white fragility backlash has seen to be a lot stronger than many expected.

I think it personally boils down to this.

Either you believe that white people have institutional advantages in society or not. The data says they do, and there is plenty of it.

If you believe in equality as liberals claim to than you need to elevate disadvantaged groups. The poor (many of whom are white), people of colour, immigrants, etc. And many see this as a zero sum game that will disadvantage those who are in power structurally. For the liberals to give up this crusade will mean giving up on a tenet of liberalism, equality.

I think the message can be refined but I think you portray it in a very selective way as to portray something as something it is not.

> First of all there is no academic debate on what murder is

Largely not because it's been broken down in a number of different categories after the debates have calmed down. There are 4 tiers of things that simplistically could be called murder, but these are not usually forced on people unless the context is relevant (like for example how someone should get treated by the justice system).

> so by it’s very nature you can’t effectively define a doctrine with one sentence

But you can jump on the cleanest parts of even that definition:      

"one's own race is superior" - simple enough        
OR    
"particular racial group is inferior" - also simple enough       

The rest is interesting, but the definition allows for calling something racist if either of those two is present. Which makes a great deal of sense, but it obviously is not describing institutional racism, which is a different thing. Rather like, say, internalized racism (which is also a problem, but would also be weird to call "racism" without any qualifiers).

 > These affect everyone of a specific racial group for historical and social reasons. By this person perpetuating racist views about a group that fuel negative perceptions and implicit bias he is actually contributing to a larger and more nefarious system of racism that affects black people.

Was with you until suddenly we shrunk to black people. Why did everyone else drop off board along the way? Even in the US context it's hard to explain why everyone would drop off, but with English being the language of the world, you kind of have to make the case for why EVERYONE else EVERYWHERE would drop off. Are American blacks truly the only ones that have ever suffered from a race based bias? I mean, that seems pretty wild a claim given more people were wiped out about 70 years ago for race based reasons than there even are black people in the US...

> Fast majorly of people? You like to throw that around a lot. 

Well. In Europe I have never, ever met a single person who thinks racism is whites against blacks. And I discuss with all of them in English, so we use the same vocabulary.

These days I live in the US and I've never actually met a real person here either who thinks that's basically racism. 

I mean I think the sample size of where the topic would have been noticeable is maybe 400-500 people? Probably a few more given the debate came up during my graduate studies in a class of 250, and nobody even there supported it (granted, business school, but one with a fair number of actual Africans, if only one black American).

I assume you understand statistics somewhat. What are the odds that a sample of that size from pretty liberal environments (Helsinki Finland, Berlin Germany, London UK, Oxford UK, Boston USA) gets zero hits? Surely you can't imagine anyone would really hide it in those?

>  If your argument is strong it should stand on it’s own without these kinds of anecdotes.

Definitions of words are not a logical thing, they are nothing but a popularity contest, though a fairly logical one with a few apparent rules for how people behave. Still, the majority gets to define anything to mean anything and that's what language essentially means - a collective agreement that certain sounds mean certain things. "Grammar" is just so much sophistication that people have tried to impose on the whole thing, and even dictionaries are followers and not leaders (just ask OED).

> I think democrats and liberals need to work on their messaging so as not to be so hostile to white people. 

It's insane to be hostile to anyone. There are plenty of ideologies that should be possible to run perfectly amicably and to the great benefit of basically everyone. One of the great things about Obama was at how good he was at articulating things in a way that people understood and didn't have to feel defensive about.

Trump is basically the opposite, but under Obama a cadre of people grew to power that really don't have Obama's sophistication with the language and who are the perfect punching bag for Trump.

> Either you believe that white people have institutional advantages in society or not. 

A very US centric point. And one reason people have such trouble on the internet with the word. Because this logic doesn't resonate AT ALL with a large number of english speaking internet (and reddit) users, just like it doesn't probably resonate super well with you that I could go on about the institutional advantages of blacks in Zimbabwe. What's that to you, after all? 

> If you believe in equality as liberals claim to than you need to elevate disadvantaged groups. 

I absolutely don't believe in equality of outcome, and consider the concept quite evil really.

That said, I believe in equality of opportunity and in a good life for as many people as possible. 

Competence should decide results, and it seems quite likely that it plays a significant role in doing just that. The problems with society aren't that competence isn't rewarded, it's that a lot of kids grow up in an environment that absolutely packs them full of competence before they reach the competitive levels.

Now you could say this is institutional, but frankly the biggest player by far seems to be family. Which is why Indian Americans are the wealthiest demographic group and why Asians are so damn overrepresented at for example Google that for them to reach their demographic "quota", the next Asian to hired at Google should be employee number 650,000 (roughly). 

Surely nobody thinks Asians run the US?

So I'm happy to equalize opportunity as much as possible, but the problem is that you can't turn a West Virginian ex-coal mining family with meth addiction in to Vietnamese entrepreneurs, or the single mother from South Side Chicago to a Chinese couple who met each other at Stanford.

How much we spend on the kids of those 4 families matters next to nothing, nor does their race (maybe some).

So I'd like focus more on figuring out what exactly to do with the societal problems that really matter like the mass imprisonment of black males. The meth/opiate addiction wave terrorizing blighted white communities. And sure, there might be institutional racism in the mix, but I think the fact that literally tens of thousands of poor white people had to get killed in a year for people to give a fuck about the opioid epidemic (if we still really do in practice...) kind of implies to me that this is far more about social class than it is about race.

@blazing-universe @charcoal-waffle Y'all are overthinking this. Racism is simple: it's prejudice + power. If you're white in America, you have power. Period. Stop making it more complicated than it is. And yeah, it's true that not every white person is racist, but the system is set up to favor us. We need to acknowledge that and work to change it.
